content:
# Luke Chapter 15 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 107-108 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_015_page_001.jpg, LUK_015_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And all the tax collectors and the sinners were drawing near to him to hear him.

**2** And the Pharisees and the scribes were murmuring, saying, "This man receives sinners and eats with them."

**3** And he spoke this parable to them, saying,

**4** "What man among you, having a hundred sheep and having lost one of them, does not leave the ninety-nine in the wilderness and go after the lost one until he finds it?

**5** And having found it, he lays it upon his shoulders, rejoicing.

**6** And having come to the house, he calls together his friends and his neighbors, saying to them, 'Rejoice with me, for I have found my sheep that was lost.'

**7** I say to you that thus there shall be joy in heaven over one sinner who repents, more than over ninety-nine righteous ones who have no need of repentance.

**8** Or what woman, having ten drachmas, if she loses one drachma, does not light a lamp and sweep the house and seek carefully until she finds it?

**9** And having found it, she calls together her friends and neighbors, saying, 'Rejoice with me, for I have found the drachma which I had lost.'

**10** Thus, I say to you, there is joy before the angels of God over one sinner who repents."

**11** And he said, "A certain man had two sons.

**12** And the younger of them said to his father, 'Father, give me the portion of the property that falls to me.' And he divided his livelihood between them.

**13** And not many days after, the younger son, having gathered all things together, traveled away into a far country, and there he squandered his property, living dissolutely.

**14** And when he had spent all, a severe famine arose throughout that country, and he himself began to be in want.

**15** And he went and joined himself to one of the citizens of that country, and he sent him into his fields to feed pigs.

**16** And he was longing to fill his belly with the pods that the pigs were eating, and no one was giving to him.

**17** And having come to himself, he said, 'How many hired servants of my father have abundance of bread, but I am perishing here with hunger!

**18** I will arise and go to my father, and I will say to him, "Father, I have sinned against heaven and before you;

**19** I am no longer worthy to be called your son; make me as one of your hired servants."'

**20** And he arose and came to his own father. And while he was yet far off, his father saw him and was moved with compassion, and he ran and fell upon his neck and kissed him.

**21** And the son said to him, 'Father, I have sinned against heaven and before you; I am no longer worthy to be called your son.'

**22** But the father said to his servants, 'Bring out the first robe and clothe him, and give a ring for his hand and sandals for his feet;

**23** and bring the fattened calf, slaughter it, and let us eat and be merry;

**24** for this my son was dead and is alive again; he was lost and is found.' And they began to be merry.

**25** Now his elder son was in the field; and as he came and drew near to the house, he heard music and dancing.

**26** And having called one of the servants, he inquired what these things might be.

**27** And he said to him, 'Your brother has come, and your father has slaughtered the fattened calf, because he has received him back in good health.'

**28** But he was angry and would not go in. And his father came out and was entreating him.

**29** But he answered and said to his father, 'Behold, these many years I serve you, and I never transgressed a commandment of yours, and you never gave me a young goat that I might be merry with my friends;

**30** but when this son of yours came, who has devoured your livelihood with prostitutes, you slaughtered for him the fattened calf.'

**31** And he said to him, 'Child, you are always with me, and all that is mine is yours.

**32** But it was necessary to be merry and to rejoice, for this brother of yours was dead and is alive, and was lost and is found.'"

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Manuscript Verification

The two Sinaiticus manuscript images (pages 107-108) were examined. Page 1 covers approximately Luke 14:34 through Luke 15:22 across four columns, with the chapter heading ΛΥΚΑΝ visible at the top. Page 2 covers approximately Luke 15:22 through Luke 16:19 across four columns, with Luke 15 concluding in the first two columns. The text is in Sinaiticus's characteristic continuous uncial script, four columns per page. Key readings confirmed from the manuscript:

- ΠΑΝΤΕΣ ΟΙ ΤΕΛΩΝΑΙ ΚΑΙ ΟΙ ΑΜΑΡΤΩΛΟΙ (15:1, "all the tax collectors and the sinners")
- ΔΙΕΓΟΓΓΥΖΟΝ (15:2, "were murmuring" — intensive compound with ΔΙΑ- prefix)
- ΤΗΝ ΠΑΡΑΒΟΛΗΝ ΤΑΥΤΗΝ (15:3, singular — "this parable," not "these parables")
- ΕΚΑΤΟΝ ΠΡΟΒΑΤΑ (15:4, "a hundred sheep")
- ΕΠΙ ΤΟΥΣ ΩΜΟΥΣ ΑΥΤΟΥ (15:5, "upon his shoulders")
- ΣΥΝΚΑΛΕΙ (15:6, unassimilated form rather than ΣΥΓΚΑΛΕΙ)
- ΧΑΡΑ ΕΝ ΤΩ ΟΥΡΑΝΩ (15:7, "joy in heaven")
- ΔΡΑΧΜΑΣ ΔΕΚΑ (15:8, "ten drachmas")
- ΧΑΡΑ ΕΝΩΠΙΟΝ ΤΩΝ ΑΓΓΕΛΩΝ ΤΟΥ ΘΕΟΥ (15:10, "joy before the angels of God")
- ΤΟ ΕΠΙΒΑΛΛΟΝ ΜΕΡΟΣ ΤΗΣ ΟΥΣΙΑΣ (15:12, "the portion of the property falling to me")
- ΖΩΝ ΑΣΩΤΩΣ (15:13, "living dissolutely")
- ΛΙΜΟΣ ΙΣΧΥΡΑ (15:14, "a severe famine" — feminine form)
- ΒΟΣΚΕΙΝ ΧΟΙΡΟΥΣ (15:15, "to feed pigs/swine")
- ΓΕΜΙΣΑΙ ΤΗΝ ΚΟΙΛΙΑΝ ΑΥΤΟΥ (15:16, "to fill his belly" — Sinaiticus reading against later ΧΟΡΤΑΣΘΗΝΑΙ)
- ΤΩΝ ΚΕΡΑΤΙΩΝ (15:16, "carob-pods")
- ΕΙΣ ΕΑΥΤΟΝ ΕΛΘΩΝ (15:17, "having come to himself")
- ΕΣΠΛΑΓΧΝΙΣΘΗ (15:20, "was moved with compassion")
- ΚΑΤΕΦΙΛΗΣΕΝ (15:20, "kissed" — intensive compound with ΚΑΤΑ- prefix)
- ΣΤΟΛΗΝ ΤΗΝ ΠΡΩΤΗΝ (15:22, "the first/best robe")
- ΝΕΚΡΟΣ ΗΝ ΚΑΙ ΑΝΕΖΗΣΕΝ (15:24, "was dead and came to life again")
- ΣΥΜΦΩΝΙΑΣ ΚΑΙ ΧΟΡΩΝ (15:25, "music and dancing")
- ΥΓΙΑΙΝΟΝΤΑ ΑΥΤΟΝ ΑΠΕΛΑΒΕΝ (15:27, "received him back in good health")
- ΤΕΚΝΟΝ (15:31, "Child" — intimate form, not ΥΙΟΣ)
- ΕΔΕΙ (15:32, "it was necessary" — imperfect of ΔΕΙ, divine necessity)

### Translation Principles Applied

1. **15:1 — "And all" vs. "Now all":** EMET renders "And all," LOGOS "Now all," PNEUMA "Now all." SOPHIA follows EMET — "And" preserves the connective ΔΕ linking this narrative to 14:35; "Now" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) introduces a modern editorial break not warranted by the Greek.

2. **15:2 — "murmuring" vs. "grumbling":** EMET and LOGOS render "murmuring" (ΔΙΕΓΟΓΓΥΖΟΝ), PNEUMA renders "grumbling." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "murmuring" preserves the LXX echo of Israel's murmuring in the wilderness.

3. **15:2 — "This man receives" vs. "This man welcomes":** EMET and LOGOS render "receives" (ΠΡΟΣΔΕΧΕΤΑΙ), PNEUMA renders "welcomes." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "receives" is the more precise rendering; "welcomes" (PNEUMA) adds a warmth beyond the neutral-to-accusatory tone of the Pharisees' complaint.

4. **15:4 — "What man among you" vs. "Suppose one of you":** EMET renders "What man of you," LOGOS "What man among you," PNEUMA "Suppose one of you." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "What man among you" preserves the rhetorical question form of ΤΙΣ ΑΝΘΡΩΠΟΣ ΕΞ ΥΜΩΝ; PNEUMA converts to a conditional.

5. **15:4 — "the lost one" vs. "the one that is lost":** EMET renders "the lost [one]," LOGOS "the one that is lost," PNEUMA "the lost one." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "the one that is lost" renders the perfect participle more precisely.

6. **15:6 — "having come to the house" vs. "having come into the house":** EMET renders "having come into the house," LOGOS "having come to the house," PNEUMA "goes home." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "to the house" renders ΕΙΣ ΤΟΝ ΟΙΚΟΝ naturally.

7. **15:6 — "for" vs. "because":** EMET renders "because," LOGOS "for," PNEUMA omits (uses exclamatory "I have found...!"). SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "for" is the standard formal causal connector, consistent with SOPHIA practice established in Luke 14.

8. **15:7 — "righteous ones" vs. "righteous persons":** EMET renders "righteous [ones]," LOGOS "righteous persons," PNEUMA "righteous people." SOPHIA renders "righteous ones" — preserving the substantive adjective form ΔΙΚΑΙΟΙΣ concisely without adding a noun.

9. **15:8 — "if she loses" vs. "if she should lose":** EMET renders "if she should lose," LOGOS "if she loses," PNEUMA "and loses." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "if she loses" renders the subjunctive ΑΝ ΑΠΟΛΕΣΗ naturally in English.

10. **15:9 — "which I had lost" vs. "which I lost":** EMET renders "which I lost," LOGOS "which I had lost." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "which I had lost" better captures the pluperfect sense.

11. **15:10 — "there is joy" vs. "there comes-to-be joy":** EMET renders "there comes-to-be joy," LOGOS "there is joy," PNEUMA "there is joy." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "there is joy" renders ΧΑΡΑ ΓΙΝΕΤΑΙ naturally; "comes-to-be" (EMET) is over-literal.

12. **15:12 — "to his father" vs. "to the father":** EMET renders "to the father," LOGOS "to his father," PNEUMA "to his father." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "to his father" is more natural in English.

13. **15:12 — "the property that falls to me" vs. "the property falling-to [me]":** EMET renders "the property falling-to [me]," LOGOS "the property that falls to me." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — the relative clause is clearer English without EMET's hyphenation.

14. **15:12 — "his livelihood between them" vs. "to them the livelihood":** EMET renders "to them the livelihood," LOGOS "his livelihood between them," PNEUMA "his property between them." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "his livelihood between them" renders ΑΥΤΟΙΣ ΤΟΝ ΒΙΟΝ with clarity; "property" (PNEUMA) uses ΟΥΣΙΑΣ vocabulary rather than ΒΙΟΣ.

15. **15:13 — "traveled away" vs. "went on a journey":** EMET renders "traveled-away," LOGOS "went on a journey," PNEUMA "left." SOPHIA renders "traveled away" (without hyphenation) — preserving ΑΠΕΔΗΜΗΣΕΝ concisely; "went on a journey" (LOGOS) adds words; "left" (PNEUMA) under-translates.

16. **15:14 — "arose" vs. "came-to-be":** EMET renders "a severe famine came-to-be," LOGOS "there arose a severe famine," PNEUMA "a severe famine struck." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "arose" renders ΕΓΕΝΕΤΟ naturally in this context.

17. **15:15 — "pigs" vs. "swine":** EMET renders "pigs," LOGOS "swine," PNEUMA "pigs." SOPHIA follows EMET and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "pigs" is the natural modern English rendering of ΧΟΙΡΟΥΣ; "swine" is archaic.

18. **15:16 — "longing" vs. "desiring":** EMET renders "desiring," LOGOS "longing," PNEUMA "longed." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "longing" captures the sustained yearning of the imperfect ΕΠΕΘΥΜΕΙ more effectively than the neutral "desiring."

19. **15:16 — "pods" vs. "carob-pods":** EMET renders "carob-pods," LOGOS "pods," PNEUMA "pods." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "pods" is sufficient in context; the botanical detail belongs in notes.

20. **15:17 — "hunger" vs. "famine":** EMET renders "famine," LOGOS "hunger," PNEUMA "starving to death." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "hunger" renders ΛΙΜΩ in its personal sense here (the son's experience), while "famine" was used in verse 14 for the broader catastrophe; PNEUMA over-translates.

21. **15:18 — "I will arise and go" vs. "Having arisen, I will go":** EMET renders "Having arisen, I will go," LOGOS "I will arise and go," PNEUMA "I will get up and go back." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "I will arise and go" renders ΑΝΑΣΤΑΣ ΠΟΡΕΥΣΟΜΑΙ in natural English while preserving the formal register.

22. **15:20 — "while he was yet far off" vs. "while he was still far-off":** EMET renders "while he was still far-off," LOGOS "while he was yet far off," PNEUMA "while he was still a long way off." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "yet far off" is slightly more formal and precise.

23. **15:20 — "he ran" vs. "having run":** EMET renders "having run, he fell upon his neck," LOGOS "he ran and fell upon his neck." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — the coordinate construction is clearer in English.

24. **15:22 — "the first robe" vs. "the best robe":** EMET and LOGOS render "the first robe" (ΣΤΟΛΗΝ ΤΗΝ ΠΡΩΤΗΝ), PNEUMA "the best robe." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "the first robe" preserves the literal ΠΡΩΤΗΝ which can mean either "first" or "best"; the ambiguity is productive.

25. **15:23 — "slaughter" vs. "kill":** EMET renders "slaughter," LOGOS "kill," PNEUMA "kill." SOPHIA follows EMET — "slaughter" is the more precise rendering of ΘΥΣΑΤΕ in the context of animal preparation; "kill" is broader.

26. **15:24 — "for this my son" vs. "because this son of mine":** EMET renders "because this son of mine," LOGOS "for this my son," PNEUMA "For this son of mine." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "for this my son" is the most compact formal rendering.

27. **15:24 — "is alive again" vs. "has come-to-life-again":** EMET renders "has come-to-life-again," LOGOS "is alive again," PNEUMA "is alive again." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "is alive again" renders ΑΝΕΖΗΣΕΝ naturally.

28. **15:25 — "Now" vs. "And":** EMET renders "And his elder son," LOGOS "Now his elder son." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "Now" marks the narrative shift to the second half of the parable.

29. **15:27 — "slaughtered" vs. "killed":** Consistent with the choice in verse 23, SOPHIA uses "slaughtered" for ΕΘΥΣΕΝ.

30. **15:28 — "would not go in" vs. "was not willing to go in":** EMET renders "was not willing to go in," LOGOS "would not go in," PNEUMA "refused to go in." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "would not" captures the imperfect ΗΘΕΛΕΝ naturally; "refused" (PNEUMA) is too decisive for the imperfect.

31. **15:28 — "entreating" vs. "urging":** EMET renders "urging," LOGOS "entreating," PNEUMA "pleaded with." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "entreating" renders ΠΑΡΕΚΑΛΕΙ with the nuance of humble appeal appropriate to the father's posture.

32. **15:29 — "Behold" vs. "Look":** EMET renders "Behold," LOGOS "Behold," PNEUMA "Look!" SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "Behold" preserves the formal register of ΙΔΟΥ.

33. **15:29 — "these many years I serve you" vs. "so many years I am serving you":** EMET renders "so many years I am serving you," LOGOS "these many years I serve you." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "these many years I serve you" is more natural English while preserving the present tense ΔΟΥΛΕΥΩ.

34. **15:29 — "a commandment" vs. "a command":** EMET renders "a command," LOGOS "a commandment." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "commandment" renders ΕΝΤΟΛΗΝ with its fuller covenantal overtone.

35. **15:30 — "prostitutes" vs. "harlots":** EMET renders "prostitutes," LOGOS "harlots," PNEUMA "prostitutes." SOPHIA follows EMET and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "prostitutes" is the natural modern English rendering of ΠΟΡΝΩΝ; "harlots" is archaic.

36. **15:32 — "it was necessary" vs. "it was fitting":** EMET renders "it was necessary," LOGOS "it was fitting," PNEUMA "we had to." SOPHIA follows EMET — "it was necessary" renders ΕΔΕΙ with its full force of divine obligation; "fitting" (LOGOS) softens the imperative.

37. **15:32 — "is alive" vs. "has come-to-life":** EMET renders "has come-to-life," LOGOS "is alive," PNEUMA "is alive again." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "is alive" renders ΕΖΗΣΕΝ concisely, consistent with the choice at verse 24.

---

*Compiled by SOPHIA in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA, from Codex Sinaiticus (4th century CE).*
